item.title: NoteVim
item.description: WTF is this? So I wanted a piece of software that was designed specifically for note taking. I wanted it to have neovim keybinds. My requirements were Full Neovim keybinds support (They are simply better as shrimple as that) Fully customizable. Lightweight. Able to support markdown for compatibility Full compatibility with obsidian to sync with my phone or windows laptop. Lightweight as in minimal startup time. So….out of the bat most markdown note taking softwares were out of the question since they didn’t have neovim keybinds.

item.title: Tmux Tutorial Pt. 1
item.description: HOIII So this is the tmux tutorial I promised last time :3 . In this tutorial I will show y’all how to set up tmux and use it in an effective and productive way…. At least that’s what I hope will happen.. So let’s begin. Installation and starting Install tmux… IDK what distro/os you are on(hopefully arch) but it should be in the package repos in your distro package manager. Try running tmux for the first time by running the tmux command in a terminal.

item.title: Tmux and Helix
item.description: HOIIIIIII !!!!!! Well I figured I’d put two Blogs into one article because it’d be easier and I needed to write longer stuff anyways ;-; Today I am writing about Helix and Tmux since I moved to Helix from Neovim despite writing an article about the editor a few days ago. BUTTTTT… the same logic from that article applies to Helix And I use tmux and Helix at the same time for my workflow so I figured I’d write about them together :3

item.title: About FKF
item.description: HOIII I am FKF. Also known as Red panda, Red or just F. I am a random 18 year old who’s interested in computers and stuff. IDK what else to say about me. You can contact me on discord the easiest. My discord username(the @ thingy) is fkf (Yes I have a 3 letter tag… insert image of flexing here)

item.title: My views on Neovim and vi keybinds in general
item.description: Hoi For all that don’t know what neovim is, It’s a fork/improved version of vim which means VI improved. Vim is a fork of the old VI editor. It was one of the two big players in the editor wars of the ancient times. read this if you want to know more about the editor wars. So….It’s a fork of a fork of a code editor from the 1970s… You might be wondering why the fuck we use it instead of something “Modern” like VS code or the jetbrains IDEs.
